text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'm going to discuss how to deploy AI apps with Streamlit, Docker, and Hugging Face Spaces. Here are just a couple of projects that might take you a couple hours or maybe a couple days on a weekend that before may have taken you weeks if not months. Things like a aentic rag system, stock prediction system, chatting with your work documents, fine-tuning LLMs, summarizing YouTube videos, predicting churn of customer data, and on and on and on. Although building projects like this and running them on your laptop is a really great way to learn, if you're trying to generate value with AI, most times this isn't enough. That's because the best way to generate value with your work is to get people to use it, to interact with it. For example, if you want to build some kind of AI SAS solution, this is going to require you hosting the application on some server that can handle multiple users. or if you're trying to build solutions for clients and stakeholders, you can't just build a solution that works on your laptop, you're going to need to bring that solution to the client so that they can actually use it and get value from it. And then finally, even if you don't have a customer or a client that you're trying to service, even making your applications publicly available on the internet and showcasing them in a portfolio is a great way to differentiate yourself whether you're looking for a full-time job or you're trying to attract clients. So the way you can give people access to your application is by deploying it. Basically making this transition from having something work on your laptop to have something working such that people from all over the world can interact with your work. While there are countless ways you can deploy code today, this can notoriously be a complicated and messy process. However, here I want to share a simple three-step way of doing this that is both beginnerfriendly but also exposes you to some key concepts and technologies that are going to be helpful in more advanced deployments. So the three-step process here is to first build a front end which we'll do via streamllet. The second is to wrap the application in a docker container. And then finally is to deploy that application. And here we're going to use hugging face spaces to do that. Let's start with step one, building a front end. But they also have a bunch of pre-built UI components. If you're trying to build a custom front end yourself, trying to figure out how to get these things working with your Python code can be a big headache. But Streamlit just makes the whole front-end process super simple and streamlined. Just to show you an example of what using Streamlit can look like, here I have an example script. The script is kind of long, so I won't go through it step by step in interest of time, but I will call out a few key features of Streamlit which make it pretty easy to use. So the first thing is I am defining the whole streamllet app in this function here called run streamllet app. And the reason it's async is just because I have a asynchronous AI agent here. But generally speaking, if you're building something that doesn't need to be asynchronous, you don't need to do this. So one cool thing about Streamlab is you can set the page headers just all using Python. No HTML needed. So you can set the page title. Streamlit has some pre-built components that we can use. A title is like the title of the web page. Then we can have markdown, which is going to be like a paragraph element on a web page, but we can put markdown format. So if we want to have like code blocks or bolded text and italicized text, we can do that very easily. Another cool feature with Streamlit is that they make it really easy to add a sidebar. So you can just add this sidebar variable here and then you can use this width syntax and then anything under this width line of code is going to be in the sidebar. So here we have a header for the sidebar. We're having a place where the user can pass in a text input. There's this session state. This allows us to maintain a set of Python variables that persist over the UI getting updated. So basically it's this data structure that we can add anything we want to here I'm going to add messages to it which is going to be a list also add input items which is going to be a list and then whenever the chat is cleared so whenever someone clicks this button the messages and the input items will be set to empty lists and then we're going to rerun the whole page doing this streamllet.rerun. And at the end we just have this syntax. So if main.py file is being run, it's just going to run the Streamlit app. So we've got the front end of our app built with Streamlit. Next thing we want to do is to wrap it all in a Docker container. Docker is a fundamental tool when it comes to hosting applications. And that's because Docker solves the runs on my computer problem. In other words, let's say I built a awesome AI app and it runs on my computer just fine. However, if I try to give the code over to a friend, a client, a stakeholder, customer, whatever it might be. This is usually not so straightforward to just get up and running very quickly. And this isn't just an issue when trying to run on someone else's laptop. Even more important is trying to get your code to run on some server that can actually make the software available to a large number of users. So your dream of having your app running all over the world actually becomes a nightmare. Just to kind of go a little deeper on what life is like without Docker. It's the same situation we described earlier where you have an app working on your laptop. We'll call it computer one. You developed it here. You got it working here and everything's great. But then when you try to go over to computer 2, you're basically reinstalling the entire codebase, the entire application from scratch, which is always going to create some kind of headache because there a bunch of dependencies you need to install. Maybe there's a specific version of Python running on your machine that's not running on the other person's machine. Maybe there are just some environment variables or libraries and other dependencies that you have installed that you have forgotten about or you just take for granted that are not available on the second computer. Usually there are a lot of headaches that come up when trying to spin up an application from scratch on a new machine. However, life with Docker, this process is a little different. So instead of just running the application on the computer, what you're first going to do is wrap the application in a so-called Docker container. Basically, you can think of this as like a mini computer running within your computer. It has all the dependencies and the files that are needed to get your app to work. If you get your app running in a Docker container on your machine, this makes it super easy to just port it over to another computer that also has Docker installed. So, you can easily spin up the new container and you can avoid the hours of pain and suffering trying to install something from scratch. There are a few key terms I wanted to define when it comes to setting up a docker container. The first is a docker file. So this is a text file that has all the instructions needed for creating a so-called docker image. Before we can actually create a docker container, we need to create this image which is basically this portable file system and configs with everything the app needs to run. In other words, the image is going to have things like all the environment dependencies, all the Python dependencies. It's going to specify what ports need to be open, what command needs to be run, right, when the container is spun up, and any environment variables that you need to get your app to work. Even though the image has everything we need to run the app, it's not actually running anything. It's kind of like a snapshot, a frozen version of everything we need to run the application. When we actually run the image, that is called a container. So this is the image actually running on a computer. It'll take requests, send back responses, and actually do what it's supposed to do. Here, I'm going to go through line by line of the Docker file I used for this YouTube AI agent project. First thing you're going to do anytime you write a Docker file is import some existing Docker image because most times you don't want to write the image completely from scratch. You want to take some image that already exists on the Docker Hub and build on top of it. So in this case, I'm going to use this Python 3.13.5 Slim, which is an image that already has Python 3.13 installed. And slim just means that it's pretty lightweight. Next, we can change the working directory from the root directory to one that's called app. This image is basically going to come out of the box with a Linux style file system which has all these like default folders in it and app is one of those folders. And then we're going to run app get which is a Linux terminal command for getting various command line tools. So we're going to update appget and then we're going to install build essential curl get and then we're going to remove this folder from our image. Then we're going to set this environment variable. So basically, Streamlit by default gathers some usage stats, which was causing some issues when I pushed this code to hugging face spaces. So we're just going to go ahead and set that equal to false. Next, what we're going to do is we're going to copy files from our local directory over to our image. So in the directory where I'm running this docker file, I have this file called pipro.toml, which has all the project dependencies. And then I also have this folder called source which has all the source code for the application. And we're just going to copy that into a new folder on the image called source. Then we're going to run pip 3 install all. So it's going to install all the dependencies from this pipro.l file. Then we're going to expose port 8501 which is the default port for streamllet. This is what's going to allow people to actually interact with our docker container when it's up and running. Then we can do a health check to make sure that the app actually works. And then finally, we can define the command that we want to be run right when the container is spun up. Here we're going to do streamlit run. And then we're going to run our streaml application that's sitting in main.py and then we define the server port we want it to run on. And then finally the server address. So this is just one example of a docker file. A lot of times you won't have to actually write these yourself. there will be some kind of template with whatever cloud platform you're trying to use, whether it's hugging face spaces or anything else. Additionally, all modern large language models know Docker and can very easily write you a Docker file if you need it.
